section: Chapter 2: Role of the Government in Health

questions.question: How is 'Health' defined in a broader sense?
questions.correct_answer: Our ability to remain free of illness and injuries, as well as mental well-being
questions.explanation: Health is not just about disease; it also depends on clean drinking water, adequate food, and a pollution-free environment.

questions.question: What is the difference between Public and Private health services?
questions.correct_answer: Public services are run by the government; Private services are owned by individuals or companies
questions.explanation: Public health centers are linked so they cover both rural and urban areas, while private services are concentrated in cities and are for-profit.
questions.source_section: Public and Private Health Care Services

questions.question: Why is the public health system called 'public'?
questions.correct_answer: Because it is meant for all citizens and is funded by the taxes we pay
questions.explanation: The government uses tax money to provide services like hospitals, police, and schools for the benefit of all citizens.

questions.question: What was the landmark 'Hakim Sheikh' case about?
questions.correct_answer: A man who was denied treatment by several government hospitals after an accident
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court later ruled that the right to life includes the right to health and ordered the state to compensate him.

questions.question: What are the health centers at the village level called?
questions.explanation: These centers usually have a nurse and a village health worker who work under the supervision of doctors.

questions.question: What is the 'Costa Rican Approach' to health?
questions.correct_answer: Providing basic health services and amenities instead of spending on the military
questions.explanation: Costa Rica is considered one of the healthiest countries in Central America because it prioritizes education, health, and clean water.

questions.question: What major step did the Kerala government take in 1996 regarding health?
questions.correct_answer: 40% of the state budget was given to Panchayats
questions.explanation: This allowed villages to plan and provide for water, food, women's development, and education effectively.

questions.question: What is the main challenge of private health services in India?
questions.correct_answer: They are expensive and often suggest unnecessary medicines or injections
questions.explanation: Since they are profit-driven, they may encourage practices that cost more for the patient.

questions.question: What are 'Communicable Diseases'?
questions.correct_answer: Diseases that spread from one person to another through water, food, air, or insects
questions.explanation: Examples include diarrhea, worms, hepatitis, and tuberculosis.

questions.question: According to the Constitution, what is a primary duty of the government?
questions.correct_answer: To ensure the welfare of the people and provide health care facilities to all
questions.explanation: The government must safeguard the 'Right to Life' for every person.

notes.title: Medical Tourists
notes.content: Foreigners who come to India specifically for medical treatment at hospitals that offer world-class facilities at lower costs.

notes.title: OPD
notes.content: Stands for 'Out Patient Department'. This is where people are first brought in and treated without being admitted to a special ward.

notes.title: Generic Names
notes.content: Chemical names of drugs that help in identifying the ingredients; they are usually much cheaper than branded medicines.

facts.fact: India is the third largest producer of medicines in the world and is also a large exporter of medicines.
About five lakh people die from tuberculosis in India every year; this number has remained unchanged since Independence.
In India, only 20% of the population can afford all the medicines that they require during an illness.
